I wouldn't be able to put in. And of course, Chloe has put in a lot of time as a therapist, obviously. And so people have different comfort levels with those type of situations. And what is the ethnic background? The Persian. Oh, okay. So there you have a lot of cultural issues. What is typical is that the children make a huge effort to become more American. And that's a huge clash with the parents who can't figure out how to deal with that. So the parents' idea of what is respect is at the end of one spectrum. And the children's idea of how they should behave is the extreme American bad behavior. They can't find the middle ground. And in terms of the wife with a husband, she probably is struggling to have the equality that an American marriage would have. And he has trouble adapting to that. So perhaps you can help her in more indirect ways to get her husband's support and acceptance. And to focus on love with the children because Persian people, really the Persian families really love each other hugely. True. And so she should focus on loving the children and getting them to love her and explain what words they can use that to her show love. And what words to her mean the opposite? Do they speak Farsi or do they speak English at home? They speak English. Even the couple, they speak English with the kids. They speak English only with their grandparents. You know, the mom and the dad, they speak Persian. Because the parents, they've been here since they were teenagers, also, both of them. Okay, so then they should understand these difficulties. And I would focus on her remembering how difficult it was for her with her parents. And that should give her some insight on how she can understand the children better. But understanding the children doesn't mean tolerating everything. Because one of the things that I've seen with Persian parents is because they just cannot conceive the level of disrespect that the children could have with them. They don't know how to establish consequences. They just believe that if they were in Iran, the children would just be naturally kind and respectful and loving. And so they're looking at the children like, where did these come from? They're from another planet that they're so mean and nasty. Yeah. It's very true because like back home, like when we were growing up, it's like you knew that you were going to respect your parents. Even if you got mad, there was no reason. No question. Right. No question. There were things you never said, words you never used. Absolutely. Yes. And these children don't have those boundaries, so she has to learn to set boundaries and consequences. Because we have to begin with your relation to the other person. And then we need to get more specific the way you communicate verbally and non-verbally with that one person. Excellent. And then, yeah, and then as a coach... It's not in some pattern. Right. And then you begin with the individual in this way and then you ask her, please note down the day after or that day, every day you will do something that shows your deep love and respect of each person and your family. For me, just quickly, since you asked all three of us from a father's perspective, I would be interested in enrolling the father to help support the mother. Often when there's a cultural difference, the father feels...there's a gap between how things are and how one feels they should be or the parents feel. The father will often disengage because he can't relate to it at all. And the mother's left having to deal with the facts of what's really happening. Exactly. Yeah, exactly. And she loses support from him because he's checked out because he would be so angry if he got involved in it. Exactly. And the father would be so outside of his comfort zone.
